summary: Conservative politician who served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom from 1979 to 1990, the only woman to hold the office and nicknamed the "Iron Lady." Won power after the strike-plagued "Winter of Discontent" defeated James Callaghan, led Britain through the 1982 Falklands War against Argentina and the 1984-85 miners' strike against Arthur Scargill's National Union of Mineworkers, survived a 1984 IRA assassination attempt at the Grand Hotel in Brighton, and was ultimately forced from office in 1990 after backlash over the "poll tax" (Community Charge), succeeded by John Major. A recurring figure in British literature and pop culture, especially as the dancing partner of Nick Guest in Alan Hollinghurst's The Line of Beauty and the title subject of Hilary Mantel's short story "The Assassination of Margaret Thatcher."

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Thatcher is identified in giveaways above all as the only woman to serve as British Prime Minister, nicknamed the "Iron Lady," and leader of the Conservative Party. Earlier biographical details cover her pre-1979 career, from a school-milk controversy as Education Secretary to a scientific background and a 1975 party leadership victory over Edward Heath.

clues.clue: The only woman to serve as British Prime Minister, nicknamed the "Iron Lady" and leader of the Conservative Party
examples: for 10 points, name this woman who became prime minister in a 1979 Conservative victory in the U.K.
for 10 points, name this late Prime Minister of England known as the Iron Lady.

clues.clue: Nicknamed "Milk Snatcher" after abolishing a free school milk program as Education Secretary under Edward Heath
examples: Name this politician whose ruthless attempt as Education Secretary to deprive school kids of nutrition led Edward Short to christen her the "Milk Snatcher."

clues.clue: Became Conservative Party leader in 1975 by defeating Edward Heath
examples: This "Iron Lady" defeated Heath to become Conservative Leader in 1975.

clues.clue: Before entering politics, worked as a research chemist and wrote a dissertation on the structure of the antibiotic peptide gramicidin S
examples: Before she effectively used girl power by funneling money into illegal paramilitary death squads in Northern Ireland, this "Iron Lady" and British PM got her start writing a dissertation on the structure of Gramicidin S.

clues.clue: As a Conservative MP, voted in favor of the Sexual Offences Act (1967), which partially decriminalized homosexuality
examples: Among the Conservative lawmakers to vote in favor of the Sexual Offences Act was this future Prime Minister nicknamed the "Iron Lady."

works.name: Rise to Power: The 1979 Election
works.description: Thatcher won the premiership in the 1979 general election, defeating incumbent Labour Prime Minister James Callaghan after a wave of public-sector strikes known as the "Winter of Discontent" undermined his government.

clues.clue: Won the 1979 general election, defeating James Callaghan after the strike-plagued "Winter of Discontent"
examples: The Winter of Discontent helped secure the election of this person, who remained in office for eleven years and was succeeded by John Major.
This Prime Minister gained office after defeating Jim Callaghan in 1979.

clues.clue: Her campaign used a Saatchi & Saatchi poster showing a long unemployment line with the slogan that the other party "wasn't working," against a Callaghan government that had formed a pact with Liberal leader David Steel and promised in its manifesto to improve industrial relations "hammered out with the TUC"
examples: This politician won an election against a candidate who formed a "pact" with David Steel and declared intent in a manifesto to "improve industrial relations that we have hammered out with the TUC."

works.name: Falklands War
works.description: Thatcher led Britain through the 1982 Falklands War after Argentina, under Leopoldo Galtieri, invaded the British-held islands; the war's outcome cemented her political standing.

clues.clue: Led Britain through the 1982 Falklands War against Argentina after Argentine forces invaded the islands
examples: The Falklands War took place during the prime ministry of this politician, nicknamed the "Iron Lady."
She presided over her nation's war with Argentina over the Falkland Islands.

clues.clue: Criticized by Labour's Ken Livingstone for her decision to send British troops to the Falklands
examples: Livingstone was critical of this Conservative leader's policies during the 1980s, including this leader's decision to send British troops to the Falklands.

works.name: 1984-85 Miners' Strike
works.description: Thatcher's government broke the year-long National Union of Mineworkers strike led by Arthur Scargill by refusing any concessions over pit closures, a confrontation marked by violent clashes at the Orgreave Coking Plant and her characterization of the strikers as "the enemy within."

clues.clue: Refused any concessions to the striking National Union of Mineworkers (led by Arthur Scargill) over pit closures, defeating the year-long strike with the help of National Coal Board chairman Ian MacGregor and calling the strikers "the enemy within"
examples: The strike was effectively broken by this Conservative PM's refusal to grant the miners any concessions. She compared the strike to the Falklands War, alleging that "the enemy within [was] more dangerous to liberty."
This prime minister defeated a year-long coal miners' strike with the help of Ian MacGregor, refusing to meet any of the miners' demands.

clues.clue: Mounted police clashed violently with picketers at the Orgreave Coking Plant during the strike
examples: Horse-mounted police charged into picketers at the Orgreave Coking plant during a campaign against this leader's government led by Arthur Scargill.

clues.clue: Reinstituted the Trades Disputes Act in response to the strike
examples: This Prime Minister reinstituted the Trades Disputes Act after facing a mineworker's strike led by Arthur Scargill.

works.name: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform
works.description: Thatcher's most consequential and controversial domestic reforms include the flat "Community Charge" (poll tax) that triggered riots and ended her premiership, the anti-LGBT Section 28, the abolition of the Metropolitan County Councils, and reforms to housing and the National Health Service.

clues.clue: Introduced the flat "Community Charge," derisively called the "poll tax," which triggered the 1990 Poll Tax Riots and led to her ouster as Conservative leader (succeeded by John Major)
examples: This leader was ousted after the intense backlash over the flat Community Charge, derisively called the "poll tax."
The Poll Tax Riots hurt the reputation of this female Conservative Prime Minister, who was succeeded by John Major in 1990.

clues.clue: Passed the anti-LGBT Section 28 of the Local Government Act and abolished the Metropolitan County Councils (including the GLC led by "Red" Ken Livingstone) via the white paper "Streamlining the Cities," sparking the "rate-capping rebellion" among local councils
examples: Municipal policies of this leader, like the abolition of MCCs outlined in the white paper "Streamlining the Cities," led local governments to organize the "rate-capping rebellion."
John Shiers led a protest of 25,000 people in opposition to this leader's anti-LGBT law Section 28.

clues.clue: Her government's Housing Act introduced "assured shorthold tenancy"
examples: The first of two Housing Acts passed under this leader introduced assured shorthold tenancy.

clues.clue: Her government commissioned the Scarman Report following the Brixton riots
examples: This leader's government commissioned the Scarman Report after the Brixton riots.

clues.clue: Received a memo after the Broadwater Farm riot that blamed the unrest on "bad moral attitudes" rather than racism
examples: A memo sent to this leader in the aftermath of the Broadwater Farm riot blamed it on "bad moral attitudes" instead of racism.

clues.clue: Reformed the National Health Service through the white papers "Caring for People" and "Working for Patients," creating an internal market
examples: This prime minister, through the white papers Caring for People and Working for Patients, reformed the NHS by creating an internal market.

clues.clue: Declared in 1987 that "there is no such thing as society," in the context of personal responsibility
examples: This politician claimed in 1987 that "there is no such thing as society."

works.name: Brighton Hotel Bombing (Attempted Assassination)
works.description: Thatcher survived an IRA assassination attempt in 1984 when Patrick Magee bombed the Grand Hotel in Brighton during the Conservative Party conference; the attack and near-miss have become a recurring subject in British fiction.

clues.clue: Survived the 1984 Grand Hotel bombing in Brighton, an assassination attempt carried out by IRA member Patrick Magee
examples: Patrick Magee attempted to kill this British prime minister, also known as the "Iron Lady".
The party was once led by this female Prime Minister and "Iron Lady," who survived a bombing attempt at the Brighton Hotel.

clues.clue: Fictionalized in Jonathan Lee's novel High Dive, in which conspirator "Dan" (alias Roy Walsh) checks into the Grand Hotel, where Freya Finch works, to prepare the bombing
examples: In that same novel, Dan assumes the name of Roy Walsh and checks in at the Grand Hotel where Freya Finch works in preparation for performing this action.
An attempt to perform this action is the subject of Jonathan Lee's novel, High Dive.

clues.clue: The subject of the title story in Hilary Mantel's 2014 short story collection The Assassination of Margaret Thatcher, in which an IRA operative succeeds in a fictional killing, ending with the line "Rejoice, fucking rejoice"
examples: The title story of 2014 Hilary Mantel short story collection is about this person's fictional assassination by an IRA operative.
that story ends with the assassin Duggan saying "Fucking rejoice" in an ironic echo of this woman.

works.name: Cultural & Literary Depictions
works.description: Beyond the Mantel assassination story, Thatcher appears repeatedly across British fiction and pop culture, most often as an actual dance partner in Alan Hollinghurst's The Line of Beauty, but also as a satirical or symbolic figure in works ranging from Salman Rushdie to the musical Billy Elliot.

clues.clue: Central to Alan Hollinghurst's novel The Line of Beauty: at a party hosted by the Feddens, a cocaine-using Nick Guest dances with her
examples: Nick Guest retreats to the bathroom for a threesome with his lover Wani and a waiter after dancing with this woman in Alan Hollinghurst's The Line of Beauty.
At a party held by his host, Gerald Fadden, Nick gets high on coke and dances "rather sexily" to the Rolling Stones with this Prime Minister.

clues.clue: Appears as a character nicknamed "Torture" by Chamcha in Salman Rushdie's The Satanic Verses, with her effigy burned at the "Club Hot Wax"
examples: An effigy of this figure is often picked for burning at the Club Hot Wax in The Satanic Verses, in which Chamcha nicknames this person "Torture."

clues.clue: In a scene from Javier Marías's novel A Heart So White, a translated conversation has a Spanish man ask her "do the people in your country love you?" instead of relaying her actual offer of tea
examples: The narrator asks this character "do the people in your country love you?" instead of relaying an offer of tea in a scene of Javier Marias's A Heart So White that translates a conversation between a Spanish man and this person.

clues.clue: Referenced in the musical Billy Elliot: sarcastically wished "Merry Christmas" at the start of Act II, while Billy's father is later attacked as a "scab" during the miners' strike
examples: This woman is sarcastically wished "Merry Christmas" at the beginning of Act II of the musical Billy Elliot, and Billy's father is later attacked for being a scab during a coal miner's strike.

clues.clue: Parodied in a Parks and Recreation scene where Leslie Knope role-plays as her, joking about losing trust in Ronald Reagan after his invasion of Grenada
examples: Leslie Knope whispers, "Oh President Reagan, my blazer popped open" during sexy historical role play while pretending to be this woman, who claimed to have lost trust in Reagan after his invasion of Grenada.

comprehensive_summary: Margaret Thatcher (born 1925) was a Conservative politician who served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom from 1979 to 1990, the only woman ever to hold the office, nicknamed the "Iron Lady." Before her premiership, she gained the nickname "Milk Snatcher" for abolishing a free school milk program as Education Secretary under Edward Heath, wrote a chemistry dissertation on the structure of the antibiotic peptide gramicidin S, voted for the Sexual Offences Act (1967) partially decriminalizing homosexuality, and became Conservative Party leader in 1975 by defeating Heath. She won the premiership in the 1979 general election, defeating James Callaghan after the strike-plagued "Winter of Discontent," aided by a Saatchi & Saatchi campaign poster declaring the other party "wasn't working" against a Callaghan government that had formed a pact with Liberal leader David Steel. In 1982 she led Britain through the Falklands War after Argentina invaded the islands, a decision criticized by Labour's Ken Livingstone. Her government confronted the National Union of Mineworkers, led by Arthur Scargill, in the year-long 1984-85 miners' strike, refusing concessions over pit closures with the help of National Coal Board chairman Ian MacGregor, denouncing the strikers as "the enemy within," reinstituting the Trades Disputes Act, and clashing violently with picketers at the Orgreave Coking Plant. Domestically, she passed the anti-LGBT Section 28 and abolished the Metropolitan County Councils via the white paper "Streamlining the Cities" (prompting the "rate-capping rebellion"), introduced "assured shorthold tenancy" through a Housing Act, commissioned the Scarman Report after the Brixton riots, received a controversial memo blaming the Broadwater Farm riot on "bad moral attitudes" rather than racism, reformed the NHS through the white papers "Caring for People" and "Working for Patients," and declared in 1987 that "there is no such thing as society." Her introduction of the flat "Community Charge" ("poll tax") triggered the 1990 Poll Tax Riots and led to her ouster as Conservative leader, after which she was succeeded by John Major. In 1984, she survived an IRA assassination attempt when Patrick Magee bombed the Grand Hotel in Brighton during the Conservative Party conference, an event fictionalized in Jonathan Lee's novel High Dive (in which conspirator "Dan," alias Roy Walsh, checks into the hotel where Freya Finch works) and the title story of Hilary Mantel's 2014 collection The Assassination of Margaret Thatcher, which ends with the line "Rejoice, fucking rejoice." She recurs throughout British fiction and pop culture: as the dance partner of a cocaine-using Nick Guest at a party in Alan Hollinghurst's The Line of Beauty, as the character nicknamed "Torture" whose effigy is burned in Salman Rushdie's The Satanic Verses, as a figure translated into a scene in Javier Marías's A Heart So White, as a sarcastic "Merry Christmas" target in the musical Billy Elliot, and as the subject of a role-play parody by Leslie Knope in Parks and Recreation.
recursive_suggestions: Arthur Scargill — leader of the National Union of Mineworkers during the 1984-85 strike, has independent bonus-part presence (B22P2, B34P3)
James Callaghan — the Labour PM Thatcher defeated in 1979, has independent presence across several bonus leadins
John Major — her successor as Conservative PM, has an independent bonus-part answerline (B33P1) and could merit his own page
Patrick Magee / Bobby Sands — the Brighton bomber and the hunger striker whose death partly inspired him, both have independent bonus-part answerlines (B10P1, B19)
Hilary Mantel — author of "The Assassination of Margaret Thatcher" and the Wolf Hall trilogy, has independent bonus-part presence (B8P3, B20P2) and no page yet
Alan Hollinghurst / The Line of Beauty — recurring independent Literature answerlines (B8P1, B17P1-2, B27P1) given the frequency of Thatcher's cameo in the novel

Ken Livingstone / Michael Heseltine / Enoch Powell — Conservative-and-Labour-era British politicians who recur as bonus-part context around Thatcher but were excluded here since the parts don't clue her directly
Facial recognition — the 'Thatcher effect' in psychology is an unrelated Social Science topic that surfaced only because of the shared name (search noise); fully excluded from this page
Laurel Thatcher Ulrich — an unrelated American historian who surfaced only due to a shared surname (search noise); fully excluded from this page
